B2Qy_xAAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Yalong Yang is an **Assistant Professor** at **Georgia Institute of Technology**, leading and contributing to the *Interactive and Immersive Visualization (IVI)* research group. His work centers on the design, implementation, and evaluation of novel visualization and interaction techniques for immersive technologies, with a particular focus on <u>Immersive Analytics</u>, <u>VR/AR</u> interfaces, and <u>human–data interaction</u>. He blends empirical user studies, systems development, and design-driven inquiry to advance how people explore and reason with complex, spatial, and multivariate data in both virtual and augmented environments.

---

## Research Areas
Yalong’s research spans several interlocking areas within visualization and HCI. He is a leading contributor to the field of **Immersive Analytics**, developing frameworks and identifying grand challenges for using VR/AR to support data sensemaking (e.g., “Grand Challenges in Immersive Analytics,” 2021). He has an extensive program of work on **geographically-embedded flow visualization**, investigating origin–destination flow maps and many-to-many flow representations both on traditional 2D displays and in immersive environments (“Origin-destination flow maps in immersive environments,” 2018; “Many-to-many geographically-embedded flow visualisation,” 2016). His papers explore representation and interaction design for maps and globes in VR (“Maps and globes in virtual reality,” 2018) and novel transition techniques for areal data in immersive settings (“Tilt map,” 2020). Yalong also investigates embodied and hybrid interactions—comparative studies of navigation strategies for 3D scatterplots, embodied vs. desktop interaction, and situated AR visualizations for sports training—tying perceptual and cognitive evaluations to practical design guidance (e.g., studies on *embodied navigation*, *situated AR visualization for basketball*, and *SportsXR*). Complementary threads include accessibility and tangible/tactile representations of network data, label placement and readability in dynamic AR scenes (e.g., RL-LABEL, 2023), and the extension of computational notebooks and collaborative visualization workflows into immersive spaces.

---

## Notable Works
- “Metaverse: Perspectives from graphics, interactions and visualization” (2022, 556 citations) — A cross-disciplinary framework articulating how graphics, interaction design, and visualization converge to shape the emerging metaverse; influential in setting a research agenda for visual construction and exploration of blended physical–digital worlds.  
- “Grand Challenges in Immersive Analytics” (2021, 278 citations) — A seminal roadmap paper that synthesizes open problems, methodological hurdles, and evaluation criteria for the Immersive Analytics community, widely cited for framing future research directions.  
- “Origin-destination flow maps in immersive environments” (2018, 142 citations) — Empirical and design-focused work demonstrating how immersive displays change the perception and interpretation of flow data; foundational for subsequent work on geospatial flows and many-to-many visualizations.

---

## Academic Background
Yalong Yang’s publication record and thesis work indicate formal doctoral research focused on the visualization of geographically-embedded flows (thesis work published 2019). Since joining Georgia Tech as an **Assistant Professor**, he has positioned himself at the intersection of **visualization**, **virtual/augmented reality**, and **human–computer interaction**, contributing highly cited surveys, empirical studies, and systems papers. His body of work (total citations ≈ 2,271) and frequent contributions to flagship venues reflect strong engagement with the visualization and HCI communities. At Georgia Tech he is affiliated with the IVI group and collaborates broadly on projects spanning immersive storytelling, sports analytics, accessibility, and hybrid/embodied interfaces. His scholarship has helped shape both theoretical frameworks (e.g., metaverse and immersive analytics perspectives) and pragmatic design patterns used by researchers and practitioners exploring next-generation interactive data environments.
